questions.question: Which infant chest compression technique is PREFERRED in the 2025 AHA Guidelines?
questions.rationale: The 2025 Guidelines preferentially recommend the two-thumb–encircling-hands technique for infants, including for a lone rescuer who can encircle the chest. The two-finger technique is no longer recommended.

questions.question: What is the correct compression depth for an infant?
options: B. At least one-third the chest depth (about 1.5 inches / 4 cm)
questions.rationale: Infant compressions are at least one-third the anterior-posterior chest depth, approximately 1.5 inches (4 cm).

questions.question: Where should you check the pulse in an infant?
questions.rationale: The brachial artery on the inside of the upper arm is used for infants, because their short necks make the carotid hard to palpate.

questions.question: If a lone rescuer cannot encircle an infant's chest, which technique may be used?
questions.rationale: When a single rescuer cannot encircle the chest, the heel-of-one-hand technique is the recommended alternative in the 2025 Guidelines (the two-finger technique is no longer recommended).

questions.question: Where on the body do you perform infant chest compressions?
options: A. Upper third of the sternum
B. Lower half of the sternum, just below the nipple line
D. On the left side of the chest
questions.rationale: Compress the lower half of the sternum, just below the nipple line, avoiding the xiphoid process.

questions.question: What is the single-rescuer compression-to-ventilation ratio for an infant?
questions.rationale: A single rescuer uses 30:2 for an infant; the ratio changes to 15:2 with two or more rescuers.

questions.question: What is the 2-rescuer compression-to-ventilation ratio for an infant?
questions.rationale: With two or more rescuers, infant CPR uses 15:2, the same as for children.

questions.question: How is the two-thumb–encircling-hands technique performed?
options: A. Two fingers on the sternum with the other hand supporting the head
B. Both thumbs on the lower half of the sternum while the fingers encircle and support the back
C. The heel of one hand on the abdomen
D. Both palms flat on the chest
questions.rationale: The two thumbs are placed side by side on the lower half of the sternum while the fingers wrap around the chest to encircle and support the infant's back.

questions.question: An infant has a heart rate of 50/min with poor perfusion despite effective ventilation with 100% oxygen. What should you do?
questions.rationale: A heart rate below 60/min with poor perfusion despite adequate oxygenation and ventilation is an indication to start compressions in an infant, as it rapidly leads to full arrest.

questions.question: How do you check responsiveness in an infant?
options: B. Tap the sole of the foot
questions.rationale: Tap the sole of the infant's foot and look for a response, rather than tapping the shoulders as in older patients.

questions.question: A lone rescuer finds an infant unresponsive and pulseless from an unwitnessed collapse. What is the recommended sequence?
options: A. Call for help and get an AED before any CPR
B. Provide about 2 minutes of CPR first, then activate help and get an AED
D. Apply an AED before CPR
questions.rationale: An unwitnessed infant collapse is likely hypoxic, so a lone rescuer gives about 2 minutes of CPR first, then activates the emergency response and gets an AED (or calls on speaker while doing CPR).

questions.question: Why is the two-thumb–encircling-hands technique preferred over two fingers?
options: A. It is easier to remember
B. It produces higher-quality compressions and better depth/pressure
C. It requires no chest recoil
D. It avoids the need for ventilation
questions.rationale: Evidence supports that the two-thumb–encircling-hands technique delivers better-quality compressions, leading the 2025 Guidelines to prefer it and drop the two-finger technique.

questions.question: What compression rate is used for an infant?
questions.rationale: The infant compression rate is 100–120/min, the same as for children and adults.

questions.question: Why is ventilation emphasized in infant CPR?
options: B. Most infant arrests are hypoxic in origin
C. Infants never have cardiac arrests
questions.rationale: Like older children, infants usually arrest from respiratory or hypoxic causes, making ventilation a critical component of effective CPR.

questions.question: An infant is unresponsive with a brachial pulse of 120/min but is not breathing. What is correct?
options: B. Give breaths at 1 every 2–3 seconds and reassess
D. Place in the recovery position
questions.rationale: With an adequate pulse but no breathing, provide rescue breathing at 1 breath every 2–3 seconds and reassess the pulse; compressions are not indicated with a good pulse.

questions.question: Which patient is considered an 'infant' rather than 'newly born' for BLS?
options: A. A baby in the first minutes after birth
questions.rationale: An infant is under 1 year of age, excluding the newly born immediately after birth (who follow neonatal guidance). A 4-month-old is an infant.

questions.question: During 2-rescuer infant CPR, which technique pairs best with the 15:2 ratio?
questions.rationale: The two-thumb–encircling-hands technique is preferred and is especially well suited to two-rescuer CPR, where one compresses and one ventilates at 15:2.

questions.question: Which combination is correct for single-rescuer infant CPR?
options: A. Two-thumb or heel-of-one-hand technique, rate 100–120/min, depth ~1.5 in, ratio 30:2
B. Two fingers, rate 60/min, depth 2.4 in, ratio 15:2
C. Adult two-hand technique, rate 140/min, ratio 5:1
D. One finger, rate 80/min, depth 0.5 in, ratio 30:2
questions.rationale: Single-rescuer infant CPR uses the preferred two-thumb–encircling-hands technique (or heel of one hand if unable to encircle), 100–120/min, depth about 1.5 inches (one-third the chest), with a 30:2 ratio.
